title: Drybulk Freight Rates Have Been Hammered Down Lately Whats Going On
section_path: Drybulk Freight Rates Have Been Hammered Down Lately Whats Going On
section_path_text: Drybulk Freight Rates Have Been Hammered Down Lately Whats Going On
summary:
Main
By Peter Lindström
Drybulk freight rates have been hammered down lately. In this article we will look at the main drivers behind the drop and whether it is reasonable to expect that these effects are temporary or if we should expect them to last into next year. Let's start by looking at how dry bulk freight rates have developed in the last couple of years.

children.title: Main
children.summary:
By Peter Lindström
Drybulk freight rates have been hammered down lately. In this article we will look at the main drivers behind the drop and whether it is reasonable to expect that these effects are temporary or if we should expect them to last into next year.

children.title: What are the underlying reasons for the steep correction the last 3 weeks?
section_path: What are the underlying reasons for the steep correction the last 3 weeks?
children.section_path_text: What are the underlying reasons for the steep correction the last 3 weeks?
children.summary: We are not sitting on all the answers, but we have some hypothesis of the main reasons for the correction in freight rates seen lately.

children.title: 1) A weaker sentiment linked to negative growth in Chinese Steel Production
section_path: 1) A weaker sentiment linked to negative growth in Chinese Steel Production
children.section_path_text: 1) A weaker sentiment linked to negative growth in Chinese Steel Production
children.summary: Chinese steel production recorded a stellar growth of 12% in the first half of 2021. However, since July, steel production has declined sequentially and recorded a negative year on growth of 20.3% in September (middle graph below).

children.title: 2) A sharp correction in commodity prices
section_path: 2) A sharp correction in commodity prices
children.section_path_text: 2) A sharp correction in commodity prices
children.summary: The active ore contract has corrected down from a level of about $200/ton in July to 93$/ton as of today. The main reason for this has been the slowdown in Chinese steel production.

children.title: 3) A rapid decrease in congestion
children.summary: The percentage of the fleet in congestion, i.e., vessels not moving at anchorages or in ports, was at very high levels in Q3, but has since been decreasing sequentially over the last three months (above right graph). The trend has been most pronounced in the Pacific where congestion is down 11% just in the last month.

children.title: 4) Charterers holding back on nominating cargoes
section_path: 4) Charterers holding back on nominating cargoes
children.section_path_text: 4) Charterers holding back on nominating cargoes
children.summary: Finally, we have the short-term effect of charterers holding back on nominating vessels. While the previous point was on new purchases of commodities not being made, this one relates to previously signed Contract of Affreightments (CoAs).

children.title: Summary
children.summary: The rapid decrease in freight rates lately can be explained by hypotheses we've discussed. While risk should be priced in for Chinese iron ore demand due to point 1, we argue that the seaborne trade of iron ore is restricted by supply not demand.
